title: Define USTs Dynamically
text: Define USTs Dynamically If you are a privileged user, you can use the Uniface Router Monitor to dynamically create or change a Uniface Server type (UST). For example, if the Uniface Router Monitor shows that too many client requests are queued up for the same shared Uniface Server, you can dynamically change the UST so more Uniface Server instances per user are allowed. To dynamically create or change the USTs: In the Uniface Router Monitor, choose URouter >Change UST . The URouter Set UST form is displayed. The fields in the form correspond to those specified in the [SERVERS] section of the Uniface Router’s assignment file urouter.asn , but do not overwrite them. Note: The Maximum fields are the only ones that can be changed for an existing UST. A value of -1 means ‘ unlimited ’. A value of -2 means ‘ do not change ’. UST name —name of an existing UST or for a new UST Maximum servers allowed —see /max Maximum idle time in seconds —see /maxidle Maximum requests allowed —see /maxreq Working directory servers —working directory for servers started under this UST definition; allowed only for new USTs; see /dir Command line switches —for servers started with this UST. Observe the following restrictions regarding command line switches: If the UST you enter matches a specific or a ‘default’ UST definition, you may not use the /dir and /asn switches. Otherwise, these switches and the switches /dnp , /ex , /shr , /su , /nosu , /pri , and /drv may be used. You may not use the /max , /maxidle , and /maxreq switches. Use the Maximum fields on this form. The /drv switch determines the role of the server to be started, for example, ORA for an Oracle database server, FIL for a file server, or S3C for a Uniface Virtual Machine server. For a database server, the database user name and password will be determined by the first client request assigned to the server. If the /drv switch is not specified, it defaults to the ANY server. Click OK . The settings for maximums take effect immediately. To revert to the Uniface Router’s assignment file, do one of the following: Choose URouter >Reload asn . Restart the Uniface Router. Related Links urouter.asn Assignment file for the Uniface Router. You can use the urouter.asn file to specify settings, paths to connectors, and Uniface Server environment definitions, such as USTs. Uniface Router The Uniface Router is a multi-threaded process (urouter) responsible for interprocess communication in Uniface applications. It listens for requests from Uniface client applications and routes them to the appropriate Uniface Server, and it passes messages between various Uniface applications. Uniface Router Monitor The Uniface Router Monitor is a Uniface Windows application that you can use to fine-tune the performance of the Uniface Router while it is running. Uniface Server A Uniface Server is a server-based process that handles requests for remote data and component execution that it receives from client applications. Acquire Privileged User Status for the Uniface Router Monitor If you have privileged user status, you can use the Uniface Router Monitor to stop and start Uniface Servers, change Uniface Router settings, change and add Uniface server type (UST) settings, stop the Uniface Router, and reload the Uniface Router’s assignment file. Define Uniface Server Types (USTs) Each UST identifies a specific Uniface Server configuration. It can be defined in the assignment file of the Uniface Router, or it can be provided to the Uniface Router by a Uniface Server that has been started manually. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
